title: 第九講
en: das Erforschliche erforscht zu haben und das Unerforschliche ruhig zu verehren.'
Epicurean view of the Gods.

Another phase of thought which seems equally modern, namely the theory that there may be gods or supernatural powers, but that nature, when once started, is governed by her own laws, and men left to their own fate, was one of the best discussed problems of the Epicureans both in Greece and in Rome. The verses which Cicero ascribes to Ennius are well known :

' I have always said and shall say that a race of heavenly gods exists, but I hold that they do not care what the human race may do ; for if they did, it would go well with the good and bad with the bad — which is not so V Chance and Purpose. Darwin.

This Epicurean concept of deity is very prevalent at the present time among what may be called the right wing of the Darwinians. Darwin, as is well known, retained the idea of a Creator, but he did not claim for Him more than that He created a few original forms, which were left to self-development into other and needful forms. He saw in the actual world, not the realisation of an ever-present Divine Thought and Will, but the result of what he called Natural Selection, Survival of the Fittest, and all the rest. Whether there is any difference be- 1 Cie. Be Div. ii. 50 : Ego deum genus esse semper dixi et dicam eoelitum, Sed eos non curare opinor, quid agat hominum genus ; Nam si curent, bene bonis sit, malis male, quod nunc abest.

HISTORICAL TREATMENT OF RELIGTOUS QUESTIONS. 227 tween the old war of all against all and the survival of the fittest and strongest, or again, between chance and natural selection, depends on a definition of terms, and no term requires so careful a definition as 'natural selection,' unless, like the Duke of Argyll, we condemn it altogether as self-contradictory. For in ordinary parlance selection requires one who selects, and if nature can select, then we have certainly a right to ask whether we may spell this selecting or discriminating Nature with a capital N. But at all events the question between chance or purpose in the Universe has been argued before by men not inferior to ourselves, and the difficulties inherent in a belief in listless gods have been discussed so fully that the experience then gained should not be ignored in reopening that old question. Here also Goethe's words deserve at least as much attention as the saying of Epicurus or Lucretius. ' God,' he writes, ' did not rest after the six days of work ; on the contrary, he continues to work as on the first day.'
Atheism.

That atheism also is not an invention of yesterday is generally admitted, though it seems hardly known at how early a date of the history of religion it comes in. In the Vedic hymns we can still watch the Aryan theogony, the very transition of natural phenomena into natural gods. But even there doubts spring up, and the ancient poets suddenly ask themselves whether after all there are such beings as the Devas. In a well-known hymn of the Rig-veda a poet expresses his doubts whether Indra, the chief god of the Vedic Indians, really exists.

The same doubt as to the real existence of such gods as Indra, that had grown into impossible beings by the accumulation of all kinds of misunderstood legends about them, occurs again and again in Indian literature. But we must remember that to doubt or to deny the existence of Indra or of Jupiter is not Atheism, but should be distinguished by a separate name, namely Adevism. The early Christians were called a0oc, because they did not believe as the Greeks believed nor as the Jews believed. Spinoza was called an atheist, because his concept of God was wider than that of Jehovah ; the Reformers were called atheists, because they would not deify the mother of Christ nor worship the Saints. This is not Atheism in the true sense of the word, and if an historical study of religion had taught us that one lesson only, that those who do not believe in our God are not therefore to be called Atheists, it would have done some real good, and extinguished the fires of many an auto da fe.
Intuitive knowledge of Gods.

And if another school of modern philosophers, baffled in their search for unconditioned knowledge, takes refuge in intuition as the true foundation of religious knowledge, this idea too is foreshadowed in the Vedic hymns. In a hymn addressed to Varuwa1, the poet begins with a confession that he has neglected the works of Varu^a, that he has offended against his laws. He craves his pardon ; he appeals in self-defence to the weakness of human nature ; he deprecates death as the reward of sin.
1 Hibbert Lectures, p. 295.

HISTORICAL TREATMENT OF RELIGIOUS QUESTIONS. 229 ' My thoughts,' he says (I. 25, 16), 'move onwards towards thee, as cows move to their pasture.' And then he exclaims suddenly, ' Did I see him who is seen by all 1 Did I see his chariot on the earth 1 Yes, he has heard these my prayers.'

In another hymn (VIII. 10), where the poet had first expressed his doubts whether the great god Indra existed at all, because it was said that no one had ever seen him, he immediately introduces Indra himself1, saying, 'Here I am, O worshipper! behold me here. In strength I overcome all creatures.'

Here we have intuition of the divine in its most primitive form. That idea, however, develops and becomes very prominent in the later theological and philosophical literature of India. As in the Old Testament, the poet in the Veda too, the Riahi, was interpreted as a seer, not as a maker. His poems were called God-given 2 ; or the gods were called the friends and helpers of the poets.
Philosophical treatment.
In later philosophical systems the question is most fully discussed, whether we ought or ought not to admit an intuition as a kind of perception, by the side of ordinary sensuous perceptions.

A few extracts from the Sfinkhya-sutras, one of the six recognised systems of Hindu philosophy, will show you how small the world of thought really is, and how exactly the same difficulties which trouble us, have troubled the minds of the gymnosophists on the banks of the Ganges. Kapila, the supposed author of the Sankhya-philosophy, admits three kinds 1 Hibbert Lectures, i> 309. - Ibid. {>. 141.

of evidence, and no more. These are called1 pratyaksha, sensuous perception, anumana, inference, and sab da, the word, particularly the sacred word or the Veda. You see therefore that this philosophy, though it is suspected of being atheistic, tries to appear orthodox. It begins by defining perception or the evidence of the senses, by the following aphorism (I. 90) : ' Perception is the discernment which portrays the form of that with which it is being brought into contact.'

The author then proceeds to defend his definition of sensuous perception against those who object that it is not wide enough, because it does not include the perceptions of the Yogins, the people who by means of fasting and other kinds of penance bring themselves to have ecstatic visions.

Kapila rejoins that these perceptions of the Yogins are not perceptions of things outside them with which their senses can be brought into contact. And if it should be said that these Yogins, in their state of exaltation, might have perceptions arising from contact with hidden or invisible things or things which exist as past and future, though not as present 2, his own definition would then be wide enough to comprehend them.

After this, Kapila proceeds to meet another objection. The critics of his definition of sensuous perception seem to have pointed out to him that his definition was not wide enough to include the ecstatic visions having Is vara, the Lord, for their object.
1 Sankliya-sutras, I. 3 ; cf. R. Garbe, Die Theorie der iwdisehi n Eationalisten , 1888.
2 Yoga-sfttras, III. 16.

HISTORICAL TREATMENT OF RELIGIOUS QUESTIONS. 231 Some Yogins must have pretended to have had such visions by means of something like sensuous perception (Yoga-sutras, II. 44-45). But Kapila declines to entertain these objections or to modify his definition accordingly, because, as he says, the existence of such a Lord has never been established (Sankhya-sutras, I. 92). From his own point of view the concept of an Isvara or Lord, as defined by the Yogins, would be self-contradictory (I. 95), and, as he points out in a subsequent chapter (V. 10), would not be established by sensuous evidence, by induction, or by revelation 1. He does not deny thereby the existence of a Lord, but only of such a Lord as the Yogins assert, namely, a being that can be reached by sensuous contact and perceived by ecstatic vision 2.
Vision in the Bhagavadgita.

How prevalent a belief in such ecstatic visions of a deity became in the religious philosophy of the Indian people, we see from the famous episode in the Bhagavadgita, where KWsh?ia appears in his true nature before the eyes of Ar^/una.

Aiv/una said 3 to Krishna, : ' I have heard from you about the production and dissolution of things, and also about this your inexhaustible greatness. O highest 1 The commentator says, fsvare tavat pratyaksham nastityanulnanasabdav eva pramawe vaktavye, te ka, na sambhavata ity arthafr This shows that my interpretation of Sutra I. 92 was right, not that of Ballantyne and Cowell, who suppose that Kapila refers to the perceptions possessed by Isvara.

Kapila restricts his remark to the Isvara of the Yoga-philosophy, and the commentator warns us against taking this as a general denial of the existence of a Lord. See also Yoga-sutras, I. 23 seq. ; and Zeitschrift dry Dmtschen Morgenltindiscfien Gesellschaft, vii. 304.
3 Bhagavadgita, translated by Telang, S. B E. viii. 92. I have abbreviated the extract and tried to make it more intelligible.
Lord, I wish now to see your divine form. If, O Lord, you think that it is possible for me to look upon it, then, O Lord of the possessors of mystic powers, show your inexhaustible form to me.'

The Deity said : ' In hundreds and in thousands see my forms, various, divine ; see wonders in numbers unseen before. Within my body see to-day the whole universe. But you will not be able to see me with merely this eye of yours. I give you an eye divine.'
Having spoken thus the great Lord showed his supreme divine form. If in the heavens, the lustre of a thousand suns burst forth all at once, that would be like the lustre of that mighty one.

Then Aiv/una said : ' 0 God, I see within your body the gods, as also all the groups of various beings ; and the lord Brahman seated on his lotus seat, and all the sages and religious snakes. I see you who are of countless forms, possessed of many arms, chests, mouths, and eyes on all sides. And, O Lord of the universe ! O you of all forms ! I do not see your end or middle or beginning. I see you bearing a coronet and a mace and a discus, — a mass of glory, brilliant on all sides, difficult to look at, having on all sides the effulgence of a blazing fire of sun, and indefinable. ... I believe you to be the eternal Being. I see you . . . having the sun and moon for eyes, having a mouth like a blazing fire, and heating the universe with your radiance. The space between heaven and earth and all the quarters are pervaded by you alone. Looking at this wonderful and terrible form of yours the three worlds arc affrighted.'

HISTORICAL TREATMENT OF RELIGIOUS QUESTIONS. 233 In Sanskrit all this sounds very grand, and when the vision is over, Krishna, assumes again his own human form. ' I cannot be seen,' he says, ' in this form by any one but yom even by the help of the study of the Vedas or of sacrifices, nor by gifts, nor by actions, nor by fierce penances. Be not alarmed, be not perplexed, at seeing this form of mine., fearful like this. Free from fear and with delightful heart, see now again that same form of mine.'

The visions of Santa Theresa and of even more modern saints are so like the earlier visions of Indian heroes that we cannot be far wrong in ascribing both to the same source and treating them both with the same indulgence.
Revelation.

In close connection with this question, the possibility of an intuitive knowledge of God, another question also, that of the possibility of a revelation, of a communication of divine or absolute truth to man, — a question so hotly discussed at present, — meets us again and again in our wanderings through the history of religion. In the Veda the inspiring influence of the gods is simply taken for granted. The gods are said to have roused and sharpened the mind of the poets \ and in the end the gods themselves were called seers and poets. As soon as the Vedic religion became systematised, and had to be defended against the doubts of friends and foes, the Erahmans elaborated an apologetic philosophy which seems to me unsurpassed in subtlety and acuteness by any other defence of a divinely inspired book. The whole of the Veda was represented as divine in ' Hibbert Lectures, p. 141 ; Rv. VI. 47, 10 ; I. 31, 1.

its origin, and therefore beyond the reach of doubt. It was not to be looked on as the work of men, but only as seen by inspired poets. It was supposed to date from all eternity, and to be so prehistoric in character that when unfortunately the names of real kings and real cities occurred in some of the Vedic hymns, as they do, they had to be explained away as meaning something quite different.
Historical traces in the Veda.
We find, for instance, in the Rig-veda III. 53, 14, the following verse :
Kim te kn'nvanti Kf'kateshu gava/*, na asiram duhre na tapanti ^ gharmam, A na/j bhara Pramagandasya veda/i, Naiftasakham maghavan randhaya naft.
This means :
What are thy cows doing among the Kikatas ? They yield no milk, they heat no kettle ; Bring us the wealth of Pramaganda, subdue, O Maghavan, Nai/casakha !

These Kika£as are evidently a tribe which did not worship Indra and which Inclra is asked to subdue. The name does not occur again in the Rig-veda, but it is said to have been the old name of Magadha or Behar on the Ganges, the future birthplace of Buddhism. According to others the northern part of Behar was properly called Magadha, while the southern portion only was called Kika^a K Whatever they were, they must have been a real race, Pramaganda must have been a real king, and Nai&asiikha, even if it meant originally, as Ludwig thinks, of low birth, must have referred to some real historical character. But all this is denied by orthodox theologians. If ' J. Bird, Historical Researches, p. 2.

HISTORICAL TREATMENT OP RELIGIOUS QUESTIONS. 235 it were so, they say, the Veda would not be nitya, eternal, or as we say, prehistoric. ' It has been said/ they argue, 'that the Veda has not a divine, but a human origin, and that in the same way as the Mahabharata was composed by Vyasa, the Ramayana by Valmiki, the Raglmvamsa by Kalidasa, so the Kanaka, Kauthuma, and Taittiriyaka, which are portions of the Veda, were composed by K&tha, Kuthuma, and Tittiri. And even if these names were only meant to signify that the families of Ka^a, Kuthuma, and Tittiri were in traditionary possession of these portions of the Veda, yet the fact that historical and real persons are mentioned in the Veda would by itself be sufficient to prove that the Veda cannot be considered as prehistoric. Now there are passages, like : " Babara, the descendant of Pravahana wished;" " Kusurubindu, the descendant of Uddalaka wished," etc. The Veda therefore must have had a beginning like all other existing things.' So far the opponent who denies the eternity of the Veda.

All this, however, is stoutly denied by (raimini, the representative of the most orthodox philosophy in India. ' The Veda,' he says, ' was the word before the beginning ; it existed before all other words, such as Ka^a, Kuthuma, Tittiri, etc., so that titles of certain parts of the Veda, such as Kanaka, Kauthuma, Taittiriyaka, etc. contain merely the names of those who hauded down the Veda by tradition. As to such names as Babara, the son of Pravahana, they must not be taken as the names of historical persons ; but Babara is really another name of Vayu, the wind, who makes a sound like babara, and whose nature it 236 LECTURE IX.
is to drive things forward, hence called pravahana (provehere). In the same manner all other historical and geographical names should be explained, etymologically, not historically.'

This is only a small specimen of what forensic theology can achieve, and could achieve long before our own time. It enables us to see both what was originally intended by such words as God-given, Godinspired, $ruti, what has been heard, Revelation, what has been unfolded, and what was made of these words afterwards. It was the sense of an over-powering truth which led to the admission of a revelation. But while in the beginning truth made revelation, it soon came to pass that revelation was supposed to make truth. When we see this happening in every part of the world, when we can watch the psychological process which leads in the most natural way to a belief in supernatural inspiration, it will hardly be said that an historical study of religion may be useful to the antiquarian, but cannot help us to solve the burning questions of the day. But this is not what I am pleading forat present. At present I want to prove no more than that an historical study of the religions of the world possesses this one great advantage, that it familiarises us with the old problems of the philosophy of religion, and fits us for a more fearless treatment of them in their modern form.
The old Problems in their simpler Form.

And by showing us the various phases through which many of these problems have passed before they assumed their present form, it teaches us another and most important lesson, namely, that in attempting to solve these problems we must not attempt to solve HISTORICAL TREATMENT OF RKLIGIOUS QUESTIONS. 237 them in their modern form only, and with all the perplexities which they present to us in their often obscure metaphysical phraseology, but that we must trace them back, as far as we can, to their first beginnings and to their simplest form.

It is with these religious problems as it is with the problems of language. Who could account for language, if he only knew the language of to-day % If we knew none of the antecedents of English, as it now exists in its 250,000 words, many of them with different meanings, many of them again having one and the same meaning, even the wisest of us could say no more than what Plato said in the Cratylus, namely that language could not possibly have been invented by man1. And now that we know by what simple process language was, if not invented, at all events produced and elaborated by man, does it lower language, because it was not invented by the gods, or does it lower man because he was not presented by the gods with a language ready made ? I believe not, and I hold the same with regard to religion. If we see with what natural feelings and simple sentiments religion began, and then follow its course till it reaches that perfect, or at all events that complete state in which we find it in later times, we shall hardly think that we degrade religion by accepting it as the most precious product of the human mind, nor shall we consider man as robbed of his dignity, be- 1 Rousseau makes the same confession. ' Quant a moi,' he writes, 'cfh;i\v des difficulty qui so multiplient, et convaincu de I'impossibilifce presque demontree que les langues aient pu naltre ei sYtablir par des moyens purement humains, je laisse a qui voudra d'entreprendre la discussion de ce difficile probleme.' See De Bonald, Recherches Philosophises, p. 117.

cause on the day of his birth the gods did not descend from heaven to present him with a religion ready made or reduced to settled creeds and finished articles of faith, but left him to grow and to learn to stand on his own legs, and to fight his own battle in the struggle for truth.
zh: 已探究可探究之事，且安然敬奉不可探究之事。
伊壁鳩魯對諸神的見解。
另一種同樣顯得現代的思想觀點，即認為可能存在眾神或超自然力量，但自然一旦啟動便由自身的法則所管轄，人類則任由自身的命運擺佈——這個問題曾是希臘和羅馬伊壁鳩魯學派最充分討論過的課題。西塞羅將下列詩句歸於埃紐斯名下，這些詩句廣為人知：
「我一直這樣說，將來也會這樣說：天上的眾神種族確實存在，但我認為他們並不關心人類種族可能做什麼；因為如果他們關心，善良的人就會遇善，邪惡的人就會遇惡——但事實並非如此。」《機遇與目的》（Chance and Purpose）· 達爾文。

這種伊壁鳩魯式的神祇觀念在當代所謂達爾文主義的「右翼」中極為普遍。達爾文如眾所周知者，保留了造物主的觀念，但他並未主張造物主做了比創造若干原始形態更多的事；這些原始形態被任其自我發展成其他必需的形態。他在實際的世界中看到的，並非一個時時在場的神聖思想與意志的實現，而是他所稱的自然選擇（Natural Selection）、適者生存（Survival of the Fittest）及其他一切機制的結果。至於是否存在任何差別¹ --- ¹ 參見西塞羅《論神之本質》卷二，第五十節：「我一直說過、也將繼續說，神祇的種族是存在的。但我認為他們不關心人類種族做了什麼事；因為如果他們關心，善人應當幸福，惡人應當不幸，但這種情況並未出現。」

在舊有的「萬物相爭」與「適者生存與最強者生存」之間，或者在機遇與自然選擇之間的差別，取決於對術語的界定，而沒有一個術語如同「自然選擇」一樣需要如此謹慎的界定，除非我們像阿蓋爾公爵那樣，乾脆譴責它為自相矛盾的概念。蓋在常用的語法中，「選擇」需要有一個進行選擇的主體，而若自然能夠進行選擇，那麼我們當然有權問到，是否可以將這個進行選擇或分辨的自然拼寫為大寫的「自然」(Nature)。但無論如何，關乎宇宙中機遇或目的的問題早已由不遜於我們的人士所論證過，而對庸碌之神(listless gods)信念所固有的困難也已詳盡地討論過，因此當我們重新開啟那個古老的問題時，從前所獲得的經驗不應被忽視。在此，歌德的言論也應當至少如同伊壁鳩魯或盧克萊修的論述一般受到重視。他寫道：「上帝在六日勞作後並未休息；相反地，他如第一日一般繼續工作。」
無神論。

無神論也並非昨日之物，此點一般已被承認，雖然似乎鮮少有人知曉它在宗教史上究竟出現得有多早。在吠陀讚歌中，我們仍能觀察到雅利安的神統論（theogony），即自然現象本身轉化為自然諸神的過程。然而即使在那裡，疑惑亦應運而生，古代詩人突然詰問自己，究竟是否真有提婆（Devas）這樣的存在。在梨俱吠陀的一首著名讚歌中，一位詩人表達了他的疑惑，不知因陀羅（Indra）——吠陀印度人的主神——是否真實存在。

同樣的懷疑——對於因各種被誤解的傳說積累而成為不可能存在之物的因陀羅（Indra）這類神祇的真實存在性的懷疑——在印度文獻中一再出現。但我們必須記住，懷疑或否定因陀羅或朱庇特的存在並非無神論，而應該以另一個獨立的名稱加以區分，即「非提婆論」（Adevism）。早期基督徒被稱為ἄθεοι，因為他們既不按照希臘人的方式信仰，也不按照猶太人的方式信仰。斯賓諾莎被稱為無神論者，因為他對上帝的理解比耶和華的概念更為廣泛；宗教改革者被稱為無神論者，因為他們拒絕神化基督之母，也不願崇拜聖人。這在真正意義上並非無神論，假如一項宗教的歷史研究只教會了我們這一課——即那些不相信我們上帝的人不應因此被稱為無神論者——那麼它就會做了些真實的善舉，並熄滅許多異端裁判所火焰的火勢。
對諸神的直觀認識。
而且若另一個現代哲學派別在尋求無條件知識的過程中陷入困境，乃求諸直觀作為宗教知識的真正基礎，這個觀點在吠陀讚歌中亦已有所預示。在一首致敬瓦魯那（Varuwa）的讚歌中，詩人起初坦承自己疏忽了瓦魯那的功業，違背了他的律法。他乞求寬恕；他以人性的軟弱為自衞之詞；他祈求免於罪惡應有的報應——死亡。
海伯特講座，第295頁。
歷史性地處理宗教問題。229 他說（I. 25, 16）：「我的思想朝向汝而進，如同牛群走向牧場。」然後他突然驚呼：「我看到了被眾人所見者嗎？我在地上看到了他的戰車嗎？是的，他聽到了這些我的禱告。」
在另一首讚歌（VIII. 10）中，詩人起初表示了他的疑慮，不知偉大的提婆因陀羅是否真實存在，因為據說從未有人見過他，但他隨即自己引入因陀羅本人¹，說道：「看我在此，祭祀者！看我就在這裡。在力量上我克服了所有的生物。」
在此我們看到神聖的直覺以其最原始的形式呈現。然而，這種觀念發展並在印度後來的神學與哲學文獻中變得極為突出。如同在舊約聖經中一樣，吠陀中的詩人，即梨師（Riahi），被詮釋為先知而非創作者。他的詩篇被稱為神所賜予的²；或者說，諸神被稱為詩人的友伴與助力者。
哲學的處理方式
在後來的哲學體系中，這個問題得到了最充分的討論：我們是否應該承認直觀作為一種感知方式，與普通的感官知覺並行存在。
梵文經典《數論經》（Sfinkhya-sutras）是印度教六大公認哲學體系之一，以下摘錄將說明思想世界確實何其狹隘，以及那些困擾我們的困難，如何同樣也曾困擾過恆河岸邊裸體苦行者們的心靈。數論哲學的據稱著者迦毘羅（Kapila）承認三種¹ ¹ Hibbert Lectures, i. 309. — Ibid. p. 141.
的證據，僅此而已。這些被稱為 pratyaksha（感知覺）、anumana（推論）與 sabda（言語），特別是聖言或《吠陀》。你因此看到，雖然這套哲學被懷疑具有無神論傾向，但它試圖表現得正統。它以下列箴言（I. 90）開始定義知覺或感官的證據：「知覺是一種辨識，它刻畫出與之接觸之物的形式。」
作者隨後著手為其感性知覺的定義進行辯護，反駁那些認為定義範圍不夠廣泛的異議者，因為該定義並未涵括瑜伽行者的知覺——這些人通過禁食及其他苦行方式使自己達致狂喜的幻象。
卡皮拉反駁說，這些瑜伽士的知覺並非對於外在事物的知覺，而他們的感官無法與之接觸。若有人辯稱，這些瑜伽士在其超越狀態中，可能具有源於與隱藏或無形事物的接觸所產生的知覺，或與作為過去與未來而非作為現在存在的事物的接觸所產生的知覺²，那麼他自己的定義將足夠寬廣，足以包含它們。
在此之後，卡皮拉繼續回應另一項異議。他對感官知覺定義的批評者似乎向他指出，他的定義範圍不足以涵蓋那些以伊濕伐羅（Isvara）、即主宰者為對象的迷狂幻象。
《數論經》第一卷第三節；參見 R. 加貝著《印度理性主義者的理論》，1888 年版。
瑜伽經，第三卷，第16經。

某些瑜伽士必然曾假裝透過某種感覺知覺（Yoga-sutras, II. 44-45）而獲得這樣的異象。然而迦毗羅拒絕考慮這些異議或據此修正他的定義，因為，正如他所言，這樣一位主宰的存在從未被確立過（Sankhya-sutras, I. 92）。從他自身的觀點看，瑜伽士所定義的伊師瓦羅（Isvara）或主宰的概念會自相矛盾（I. 95），而且，如他在隨後的章節所指出的（V. 10），既不能由感覺證據、歸納法或啟示所確立。他並非據此否認主宰的存在，而是否認瑜伽士所主張的那樣一位主宰的存在，即一個可以透過感覺接觸而到達、透過狂喜中的異象而被感知的存有。
《薄伽梵歌》中的視象
從《薄伽梵歌》中的著名篇章可以看出，對於提婆之狂喜異象的信念在印度人民的宗教哲學中是多麼普遍——在那段篇章中，奎師那（Kṛṣṇa）在阿周那（Arjuna）的眼前顯現了他的真實本性。

阿周那對克里希那說：「我已從你那裡聽聞了萬物的生成與消融，也聽聞了你這無窮無盡的偉大。至高者啊！」評註家說：「伊濕瓦羅實則無有直接知覺（*Isvare tavat pratyaksham nasti*），因此只能以言語作為量（*anumanasabdav eva pramawe vaktavye*），然而這些也不可能成立（*te ka, na sambhavata ity artha*）。」此足以證明我對《經文》I. 92 的詮釋乃為正確，而非巴蘭泰因與考威爾之說，彼二人以為迦毘羅乃指伊濕瓦羅所具有的知覺。
卡比拉將其評論侷限於瑜伽哲學中的伊斯瓦拉（Isvara），而註釋者警告我們不要將此視為對主宰存在之普遍性的否定。參見《瑜伽經》(Yoga-sutras) 卷一第二十三頌及以下；以及《德國東方學會雜誌》(Zeitschrift der Deutschen Morgenländischen Gesellschaft) 卷七第三○四頁。
3 《薄伽梵歌》，德朗（Telang）譯，《聖書東方文庫》第八卷，第92頁。本人曾略縮節選文並力求使其更易理解。
主啊，我現在願意看見你的神聖形相。倘若，主啊，你認為我有可能注視它，那麼，具有神祕力量者的主啊，請向我展現你無窮盡的形相。
神祇說：「在數百數千中，看見我的形貌，種種而神聖；看見未曾見過的奇蹟於眾數之中。在我的身軀之內，今日看見整個宇宙。但你將無法僅憑你的肉眼看見我。我賜予你一隻神聖的眼睛。」
偉大的主如此言畢，展現其至高無上的神聖形相。若千陽之光輝同時於諸天中爆發，其耀光將如同那位至強者之光輝。

然後阿爾朱那說：『哦神啊，我在你的身體內看見諸神，以及各種眾生的各個族群；以及坐在蓮花座上的梵天主，以及所有的賢者和宗教的蛇。我看見你擁有無數的形相，具備眾多的手臂、胸膛、嘴巴，以及四面八方的眼睛。而且，哦宇宙之主！哦你這萬般形相者！我看不見你的終端或中間或起始。我看見你身著冠冕和權杖，以及持著圓盤——一團榮耀的聚集，四面八方光彩奪目，難以直視，四面八方都散發著熾熱日火的光輝，而且不可名狀。...我相信你就是永恆的存在。我看見你...以太陽和月亮為眼睛，以熾烈的火焰為嘴巴，用你的光輝加熱宇宙。天地之間的空間以及所有的方向都單獨被你所充滿。看著你這奇妙而可怕的形相，三界都感到恐懼。』
宗教問題的歷史考察 233 在梵文中，這一切聽起來都極其宏偉，當這一景象結束時，克里希那假定他再度恢復自己的人類形貌。他說：「沒有人能夠用這種形貌看見我，即便藉由吠陀的研習、祭祀、禮物、行為或嚴厲的苦行也不能。不要驚懼，不要困惑於看見我這樣令人恐懼的形貌。拋卻恐懼，以欣悅的心，再次看見我同樣的形貌吧。」
聖特蕾莎的幻象，乃至更晚近聖者的幻象，與早期印度英雄的幻象相似至極，以至於我們若將兩者都歸溯於同一根源，並以相同的寬容態度對待兩者，應該不會遠離事實。
啟示。

在與此問題密切相連的地方，直觀認識上帝的可能性，以及另一個問題——神聖啟示的可能性、神聖或絕對真理向人類的溝通——這一個當今激烈爭論的問題——在我們遊歷宗教史的過程中一再出現。在《吠陀》中，諸神的啟發影響力被簡單地視為理所當然。據說諸神喚醒並磨礪了詩人的心智；到了最後，諸神本身也被稱為先知和詩人。一旦吠陀宗教被系統化，且不得不對抗友敵的懷疑而進行辯護時，婆羅門人便闡發出一套道歉性哲學，其細微與敏銳之處似乎未曾為任何其他對神聖啟示之書的辯護所超越。整個《吠陀》被表述為神聖的。¹ --- ¹ *Hibbert Lectures*, p. 141; *Rv.* VI. 47, 10; I. 31, 1.
其起源，因而超越了懷疑的範圍。它不應被視為人類的著作，而只能被視為受靈感啟示的詩人所見之物。它被認為源自永恆之初，且其性質如此久遠以至於史前，以至於當不幸地一些實在的君王和實在的城市名稱出現在某些吠陀讚歌中時——正如它們所做的那樣——它們必須被曲解為意指全然不同的含義。

在吠陀文獻中，我們可以發現某些歷史痕跡，這些痕跡不僅揭示了宗教思想的演變，也反映了雅利安人與其他民族接觸時所產生的影響。吠陀頌歌中提及的河流、山脈與地名，為我們提供了地理與遷徙的線索；而其中對神祇的稱呼與描述，則暗示了信仰體系的發展階段。例如，*梨俱吠陀*（Rig-Veda）中對特尤斯（Dyaus）與提婆（Deva）的區別，顯示了從自然崇拜向更抽象神靈概念的過渡。這些痕跡並非系統性的歷史記載，而是透過比較神話學（comparative mythology）與語言科學（the science of language）的分析，得以重構的片段。穆勒（Müller）強調，吠陀中的這些元素，如同「語言的疾病」（a disease of language）一般，反映了早期人類試圖以有限語言表達無限者（the Infinite）時所產生的隱喻與誤解。因此，吠陀不僅是宗教文獻，更是理解單一神教（henotheism）與輪換主神教（kathenotheism）等早期信仰形式的關鍵材料。
例如，我們在《梨俱吠陀》第三卷第五十三讚歌第十四節中，發現如下詩句：
我注意到提供的「英文原文」實際上是梵文文本，而非英文。根據您的指示第2點，我只應翻譯英文原文，梵文文本不在翻譯範圍內。 若您需要此梵文段落的翻譯，請提供對應的英文翻譯版本，我將按照您的規則進行翻譯。
這意味著：
你的母牛在基卡塔人中做什麼？它們不產奶，不加熱水壺；為我們帶來普拉瑪甘達的財富，征服吧，啊摩竭婆，納伊迦薩卡！

基卡塔人顯然是一個不崇拜因陀羅的部族，因陀羅被祈求去征服他們。這個名字在《梨俱吠陀》中沒有再出現，但據說它曾是恆河邊上摩揭陀或比哈爾的舊稱，即佛教未來的誕生地。據其他說法，比哈爾的北部才是真正的摩揭陀，而南部才被稱為基卡塔。無論他們究竟是誰，他們必然是真實存在的種族，頻羅磨甘陀必然是真實存在的國王，而那羅辛訶，即使如路德維希所認為的那樣，原本意指出身低賤，也必然指涉到某個真實的歷史人物。但這一切都被正統神學家所否定。¹ ¹ J. Bird, Historical Researches, p. 2.

他們說，若果真如此，吠陀就不會是常恆的（nitya），或者如我們所說的，先史的。他們辯稱：「據說吠陀並非出於神聖的起源，而是人為的起源，正如摩訶婆羅多由毗耶娑（Vyasa）所作，羅摩衍那由瓦爾彌基（Valmiki）所作，王統紀（Raghuvaṃśa）由迦梨陀娑（Kalidasa）所作一樣，那麼卡那卡（Kanaka）、考圖瑪（Kauthuma）與泰提利耶卡（Taittiriyaka）——這些都是吠陀的組成部分——也分別由卡他（Kātha）、庫圖瑪（Kuthuma）與蒂蒂里（Tittiri）所作。即使這些名字僅是用來表示卡他家族、庫圖瑪家族與蒂蒂里家族傳統地掌持著吠陀的這些部分，然而吠陀中提及歷史性的、真實的人物這一事實本身就足以證明，吠陀不能被認為是先史的。吠陀中存在著這樣的段落，例如：『巴巴拉（Babara），普拉瓦哈那（Pravahana）的後裔希望……』；『庫蘇魯賓度（Kusurubindu），烏達拉卡（Uddalaka）的後裔希望……』等等。因此，吠陀必然有其開始，正如所有其他既存的事物一樣。」這便是否定吠陀常恆性的反對者的論點。

然而，這一切卻遭到印度最正統哲學的代表人物毘耶娑（Vyaśa）的堅決否認。他說：「吠陀是太初之言；它存在於一切其他言詞之前，如迦（Ka）、庫圖摩（Kuthuma）、蒂蒂利（Tittiri）等，因此吠陀某些部分的名稱，如迦納卡（Kanaka）、考圖摩（Kauthuma）、泰提利耶卡（Taittiriyaka）等，僅僅包含了那些以傳統方式傳承吠陀的人的名字。至於巴巴拉（Babara）、普拉瓦哈那之子（the son of Pravahana）這樣的名字，不可以理解為歷史人物的名稱；而是巴巴拉實際上是風神瓦尤（Vayu）的另一個名字，瓦尤發出如巴巴拉般的聲音，其本質
是要推動事物向前，因此被稱為 pravahana（provehere）。同樣地，所有其他歷史地名和地理名稱都應該用詞源學而非歷史學來解釋。

這只是法庭神學所能成就之事的一個小樣本，而且在我們這個時代之前早已能夠成就。它使我們能夠看清，諸如「天賜的」（God-given）、「天啟的」、श्रुति（śruti，即所聽聞者）、「啟示」（Revelation，即所展開者）這樣的詞語最初的本義為何，以及這些詞語後來如何被理解。正是對於一種勢不可擋的真理的領悟，導致人們承認啟示的存在。然而，在開始之時，真理導致了啟示；但不久之後，人們就開始假設啟示能夠製造真理。當我們看到這種情況在世界各地發生，當我們能夠觀察導致超自然啟示信念的心理學過程，而這過程以最自然的方式發生時，實在很難說一部宗教的歷史研究也許對古物學家有用，卻無法幫助我們解決當今迫在眉睫的問題。然而，這並非我目前所要主張的。目前我只想證明一點，即世界諸宗教的歷史研究擁有這樣一項重大的優勢，就是它使我們熟悉宗教哲學的古老課題，並使我們能更無所畏懼地面對這些課題的現代形式。
簡化形式中的古老問題
透過展示許多這些問題在假定其現代形式之前所經歷的各個階段，它教導我們另一項最為重要的教訓，即：在嘗試解決這些問題時，我們不應該僅僅以其現代形式來解決它們，以及它們在往往晦澀難懂的形而上學語彙中所呈現的所有困難，而是我們必須盡我們所能地將它們追溯到它們最初的開端以及它們最簡單的形式。

宗教問題與語言問題的情況如出一轍。倘若一個人只認識當今的語言，他又如何能夠解釋語言呢？如果我們對英語的前身毫無所知，而英語現今已有二十五萬個詞彙，其中許多詞彙具有不同的含義，許多詞彙則共享同一個意思，即使是最聰慧的人也只能重複柏拉圖在《克拉底魯篇》(Cratylus) 中所說的話，亦即語言不可能是人類所發明的¹。然而，既然我們現已知曉語言是經由何種簡單的過程，倘若非人類所發明，至少也是由人類所產生並精心發展出來的，這難道會因為語言並非由諸神所發明而貶低語言本身，或因為人類未曾獲得諸神所預製的現成語言而貶低人類本身嗎？我認為並不會。我對宗教的看法亦然。若我們看到宗教如何源於自然的情感與樸素的情緒，進而跟隨其發展過程，直至它達到那種完美的，或至少說是圓滿的狀態——正如我們在後世所見的那般——我們便幾乎不會認為接納宗教作為人類心靈最寶貴的產物會有所貶低，也不會認為人類因未曾被諸神直接賦予現成的宗教而遭到尊嚴的剝奪。 ¹ 盧梭亦提出相同的看法。他寫道：「至於我，面對日益增多的困難，並且確信—— 此點幾乎已被證明——語言不可能通過純粹人為的手段而產生並確立，我將此困難的問題留給願意探討者。」見德‧博納爾德《哲學研究》(De Bonald, Recherches Philosophises)，第 117 頁。
在他出生的那一天，眾神並未從天堂降臨，向他贈送一個現成的宗教，或一套固定的信條與完整的信仰條文，而是任憑他自行成長，學會自立自強，在追求真理的鬥爭中自力奮戰。
